vignette_text: The forest burns down if either a match is dropped or lightning strikes. A match is dropped and lightning strikes.
title: Forest Fire disjunctive

vignette_text: The forest burns down if a match is dropped and lightning strikes. A match is dropped and lightning strikes.
title: Forest Fire conjunctive

vignette_text: Suzy throws a rock at a bottle. Billy throws slightly after, but before Suzy's rock hits. Suzy's rock hits the bottle and shatters it. Billy's rock would have hit if Suzy's had not.
title: Rock throwing

vignette_text: Suzy throws a rock at a bottle, while Billy idly stands by with a rock in his hand, having no intention to throw it. Suzy’s rock hits the bottle, at which point it shatters.
title: Only Suzy throws

vignette_text: Assassin poisons Victim’s coffee. Bodyguard is in possession of an antidote that is capable of neutralizing the poison, but she refrains from administering it to Victim. Victim dies from the poison. Victim would not have died if the antidote had been administered or if the coffee had not been poisoned.
title: Ommitted prevention

vignette_text: Assassin is in possession of a lethal poison, but has a last-minute change of heart and refrains from putting it in Victim’s coffee. Bodyguard puts antidote in the coffee, which would have neutralized the poison. Victim drinks the coffee and survives.
title: Bogus prevention

vignette_text: Assassin poisons Victim’s coffee. Bodyguard administers an antidote that neutralizes the poison. Victim survives.
title: Prevention

vignette_text: A prisoner dies either if Alice loads Bob’s gun and Bob shoots, or if Carlos loads and shoots his gun. In the actual context, Alice loads Bob’s gun, Bob does not shoot, but Carlos does load and shoot his gun, so that the prisoner dies.
title: Loader

vignette_text: A lamp is controlled by three switches, each of which has three possible positions: right, middle, left. The lamp switches on iff two or more switches are in the same position. In fact, the first switch is in the right position, the second in the left, and the third in the left.
title: Combination Lamp

vignette_text: The forest burns down if either a match is dropped or lightning strikes. There are three possible mechanisms for the forest to catch on fire: either lightning strikes and the match isn't dropped, or lightning doesn't strike and the match is dropped, or both the lightning strikes and the match is dropped. The forest fire happens if either of these three scenarios happens. In the actual context, lightning strikes and the match is dropped.
title: Firest Fire mechanisms

vignette_text: A ranch has five individuals: Cowboy, Ranger, Wrangler, and two Hands. Everyone votes either for staying around the campfire, or for going on a round-up. A complicated rule is used to decide the outcome: (a) if Cowboy and Ranger agree, then the outcome is whatever the Ranger voted for; (b) if Ranger differs from the other four, then the outcome is what the Ranger voted for; and (c) otherwise, majority rules. Suppose Cowboy and Ranger vote to go on a round-up, and Wrangler and the two Hands vote to stay at the campfire (and so the outcome is to go on a round-up).
title: Campfire vote

vignette_text: Alice, Bob, Carlos, Dirk and Eric fire at and simultaneously hit a target that will fall over if at least 3 bullets hit it. The target falls over.
title: Voting

vignette_text: The engineer is standing by a switch in the railroad tracks. A train approaches in the distance. She flips the switch, so that the train travels down the right-hand track instead of the left. Since the tracks reconverge up ahead, the train arrives at its destination all the same.
title: The Engineer

vignette_text: The engineer is standing by a switch in the railroad tracks. A train approaches in the distance. She flips the switch, so that the train travels down the right-hand track instead of the left. Since the tracks reconverge up ahead, the train arrives at its destination all the same.
title: The Engineer

vignette_text: The engineer is standing by a switch in the railroad tracks. A train approaches in the distance. She flips the switch, so that the train travels down the right-hand track instead of the left. Since the tracks reconverge up ahead, the train arrives at its destination all the same.
title: The Engineer

vignette_text: Suppose that a dog bites Jim’s right hand. Jim was planning to detonate a bomb, which he normally would do by pressing the button with his right forefinger. Because of the dog bite, he presses the button with his left forefinger. The bomb still goes off.
title: Dog Bite

vignette_text: Assistant Bodyguard puts a harmless antidote in Victim’s coffee. Buddy then poisons the coffee, using a poison that is normally lethal, but which is countered by the antidote. Buddy would not have poisoned the coffee if Assistant had not administered the antidote first. Victim drinks the coffee and survives.
title: Careful Poisoning

vignette_text: Two votes are cast for a measure. The votes are summed by a machine and the measure passes iff it receives at least one vote.
title: Voting machine
Two paths
description: The value of A is set by the context; we have the equations B = A, C = A, and D = B ∨ C. In the actual context A = 1, so B = C = D = 1.

vignette_text: Carlos, who lives far away in Australia, doesn't water Alice's plant, and the plant then dries up and dies.
title: Default Omission
notes: this model is confusing because it also includes individualism vs. Collectivism

vignette_text: Barry promises to water Alice's plant, doesn't water it, and the plant then dries up and dies.
title: Deviant Omission

vignette_text: Suppose Suzy goes away on vacation, leaving her favorite plant in the hands of Billy, who has promised to water it. Billy fails to do so. The plant dies—but would not have, had Billy watered it. But Carlos, who lives far away in Australia, also failed to water Suzy’s plant. And, had he done so, it would not have died.
title: Double Omission

vignette_text: A boulder is dislodged, and begins rolling ominously toward Hiker. Before it reaches him, Hiker sees the boulder and ducks. The boulder sails harmlessly over his head with nary a centimeter to spare. Hiker survives his ordeal.
title: Boulder
origin: early draft of Hall - Two Concepts

title: Extended double prevention
description: F initiates a threat to E: for if nothing stops B from stimulating D, then E won’t fire. F also cancels this threat, by way of C. So the little four-neuron network F–B–C–D might aptly be called a ‘‘short-circuit’’, with respect to E.

vignette_text: Assassin poisons Victim’s coffee. Victim drinks it and dies. If Assassin hadn’t poisoned the coffee, Backup would have, and Victim would have died anyway. Victim would not have died if there had been no poison in the coffee.
title: Backup Assassin

vignette_text: Suzy is piloting a bomber on a mission to blow up an enemy target, and Billy is piloting a fighter as her lone escort. Along comes an enemy fighter plane, piloted by Enemy. Sharp-eyed Billy spots Enemy, zooms in, pulls the trigger, and Enemy’s plane goes down in flames. Suzy’s mission is undisturbed, and the bombing takes place as planned.
title: Double prevention

vignette_text: Suzy is piloting a bomber on a mission to blow up an enemy target, and Billy and Hillary both pilot a fighter as escort each. Along comes an enemy fighter plane, piloted by Enemy. Sharp-eyed Billy spots Enemy, zooms in, pulls the trigger, and Enemy’s plane goes down in flames. If Billy hadn't shot, Hillary would've. Suzy’s mission is undisturbed, and the bombing takes place as planned.
title: Double prevention

vignette_text: The family goes to bed while a thief plans to enter their house, and police patrols the surrounding area. Police catches the thief, preventing him from entering the house. The family sleeps peacefully through the night.
title: Double prevention

vignette_text: The family goes to bed while no thief plans to enter their house, and police patrols the surrounding area. Police would've caught any thief in the vicinity. The family sleeps peacefully through the night.
title: Non-existent threat

vignette_text: Assassin puts poison in Victim’s coffee. The poison, if not counteracted, causes a painful death. Bodyguard puts a weak antidote in Victim’s coffee. The antidote is not strong enough to neutralize the poison and hence save Victim’s life, but at least it is strong enough to render Victim’s death painless.
title: Affecting

vignette_text: Assassin puts poison in the coffee. Bodyguard responds by putting antidote in the coffee, which neutralizes the poison. (The antidote is harmless when taken alone.) Victim drinks the coffee and survives. If Assassin hadn’t poisoned the coffee, Bodyguard would not have administered the antidote. If Bodyguard hadn’t administered the antidote, Victim would have died from the poison.
title: Counterexample to Transitivity

vignette_text: Suppose that there was a heavy rain in April and electrical storms in the following two months, and in June the lightning took hold. If it had not been for the heavy rain in April, the forest would have caught fire in May.
title: April rains

vignette_text: Suppose that there was a heavy rain in April and electrical storms in the following two months, and in June the lightning took hold. If it had not been for the heavy rain in April, the forest would have caught fire in May.
title: April rains
taken_from: HP 2005, Ex. 4.1, separate variables M and J

vignette_text: Suzy and Billy both pick up rocks and throw them at a bottle. Suzy’s rock gets there first, shattering the bottle. Since both throws are perfectly accurate, Billy’s would have shattered the bottle had it not been preempted by Suzy’s throw.
title: Rock bottle with time

vignette_text: Billy, having stayed out in the cold too long throwing rocks, contracts a serious but nonfatal disease. He is hospitalized and treated on Monday, so is fine Tuesday morning.
title: Monday treatment

vignette_text: Billy, having stayed out in the cold too long throwing rocks, contracts a serious but nonfatal disease. Doctor forgets to treat him and Billy is sick on Tuesday.
title: Monday treatment omission

vignette_text: Suppose that Monday’s doctor is reliable, and administers the medicine first thing in the morning, so that Billy is fully recovered by Tuesday afternoon. Tuesday’s doctor is also reliable and would have treated Billy if Monday’s doctor had failed to . . . And let us add a twist: one dose of medication is harmless, but two doses are lethal.
title: Deadly Monday treatment

vignette_text: Suzy and Billy throw a rock at a bottle, but Suzy’s hits first. Now suppose that there is a noise which causes Suzy to delay her throw slightly, but that she still throws before Billy.
title: Noisy rock bottle

vignette_text: Fred has his finger severed by a machine at the factory. Fortunately, Fred is covered by a health plan. He is rushed to the hospital, where his finger is sewn back on. A month later, the finger is fully functional.
title: Finger severed

vignette_text: Fred has his finger severed by a machine at the factory. Fortunately, Fred is covered by a health plan. He is rushed to the hospital, where his finger is sewn back on. A month later, the finger is fully functional. Larry the Loanshark may be waiting outside the factory with the intention of cutting off Fred’s finger as a warning to him to repay his loan quickly. In the actual situation, Larry is not waiting and Larry does not cut off Fred’s finger.
title: Larry the Loanshark
description: Let LL represent whether or not Larry is waiting and let LC represent whether Larry cuts off Fred’s finger. If Larry cuts off Fred’s finger, he will throw it away, so Fred will not be able to get it sewn back on.

vignette_text: Major and sergeant stand before corporal, and both shout ‘Charge!’. The corporal charges. Orders from higher ranking soldiers trump those of lower rank, so if the major had shouted ‘Halt’ the corporal would not have charged.
title: Command

vignette_text: A ball is caught by a fielder. A little further along its path there is a solid wall and, beyond that, a window.
title: Window wall

vignette_text: Two two-state switches are wired to an electrode. The switches are controlled by Alice and Beth respectively, and the electrode is attached to Carlos. Alice has the first option to flip her switch. Beth has the second option to flip her switch. The electrode is activated and shocks Carlos iff both switches are in the same position. Beth wants to shock Carlos, and so flips her switch iff Alice does. In the actual context, Alice flips her switch.
title: Shock

vignette_text: A lamp is controlled by three switches, each of which has three possible positions: left, middle, right. The switches are connected to three detectors, each of which is activated iff no switch is in position (left, middle, right) respectively. The lamp switches on iff some detector is activated. In fact, the switches are in the following positions: the first right, the second left, and the third left. The middle-detector is activated, and the lamp turns on.
title: Fancy lamp

vignette_text: Alex, an imperfect marksman, is about to fire at the target, but his aim is too low. Bob standing at the back of the crowd, could push his way through to Alex and lift the rifle barrel just the right amount, but Bob does no such thing. Alex’s bullet misses the bullseye.
title: Marksman omission

vignette_text: Alex, the perfect marksman, aims at the target, but fails to cock his gun, and pulls the trigger. The gun does not fire and the target is untouched.
title: Unloaded gun

vignette_text: A gun has a safety mechanism: the gun will not fire unless the hammer is cocked and the round is chambered and the trigger is pulled. Pulling the trigger causes a round to be chambered but prevents the hammer from being cocked. The trigger is pulled. The gun does not fire.
title: Safe gun

vignette_text: Alex and Bob, both perfect marksmen, shoot at the target at almost the same time. The ejected shell from Alex’s pistol deflects Bob’s bullet, which would otherwise have hit the target bullseye. Alex’s bullet hits the bullseye.
title: Deflected Bullet

vignette_text: Alex and Beth, both perfect marksmen, pull their triggers on similar guns at the same time. Beth loaded her rifle and hits the bullseye. Alex has forgotten to load his rifle.
title: Two loaders

vignette_text: A woman takes birth control pills which prevent a pregnancy that, had it occurred, would have caused the actual thrombosis caused by taking the birth control pills.
title: Thrombosis

vignette_text: Suppose that two companies both dump pollutant into the river. Company A dumps 100 kilograms of pollutant; company B dumps 60 kilograms. This causes the fish to die. Biologists determine that 50 kilograms of pollutant sufficed to cause the fish to die. Which company is the cause of the fish dying?
title: Pollutant (k=50)

vignette_text: Suppose that two companies both dump pollutant into the river. Company A dumps 100 kilograms of pollutant; company B dumps 60 kilograms. This causes the fish to die. Biologists determine that 80 kilograms of pollutant sufficed to cause the fish to die. Which company is the cause of the fish dying?
title: Pollutant (k=80)

vignette_text: Suppose that two companies both dump pollutant into the river. Company A dumps 100 kilograms of pollutant; company B dumps 60 kilograms. This causes the fish to die. Biologists determine that 120 kilograms of pollutant sufficed to cause the fish to die. Which company is the cause of the fish dying?
title: Pollutant (k=120)

vignette_text: Suppose that two companies both dump pollutant into the river. Company A dumps 100 kilograms of pollutant; company B dumps 60 kilograms. This causes the fish to die. Biologists determine that 80 kilograms of pollutant sufficed to cause the fish to die. Which company is the cause of the fish dying?
title: Pollutant (k=80)
